title: Godiva
content:
I waited for the train at Coventry;
I hung with grooms and porters on the bridge,
To match the three tall spires; and there I shaped
The cityï¿½s ancient legend into this:ï¿½
Not only we, the latest seed of Time,
New men, that in the flying of a wheel
Cry down the past, not only we, that prate
Of rights and wrongs, have loved the people well,
And loathed to see them overtaxï¿½d; but she
Did more, and underwent, and overcame,
The woman of a thousand summers back,
Godiva, wife to that grim Earl, who ruled
In Coventry: for when he laid a tax
Upon his town, and all the mothers brought
Their children, clamouring, ï¿½If we pay, we starve!ï¿½
She sought her lord, and found him, where he strode
About the hall, among his dogs, alone,
His beard a foot before him, and his hair
A yard behind. She told him of their tears,
And prayï¿½d him, ï¿½If they pay this tax, they starveï¿½.
Whereat he stared, replying, half-amazed,
ï¿½You would not let your little finger ache
For such as these?ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½But I would die,ï¿½ said she.
He laughï¿½d, and swore by Peter and by Paul;
Then fillipï¿½d at the diamond in her ear;
ï¿½O ay, ay, ay, you talk!ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½Alas!ï¿½ she said,
ï¿½But prove me what it is I would not do.ï¿½
And from a heart as rough as Esauï¿½s hand,
He answerï¿½d, ï¿½Ride you naked throï¿½ the town,
And I repeal itï¿½; and nodding as in scorn,
He parted, with great strides among his dogs.
So left alone, the passions of her mind,
As winds from all the compass shift and blow,
Made war upon each other for an hour,
Till pity won. She sent a herald forth,
And bad him cry, with sound of trumpet, all
The hard condition; but that she would loose
The people: therefore, as they loved her well,
From then till noon no foot should pace the street,
No eye look down, she passing; but that all
Should keep within, door shut, and window barrï¿½d.
Then fled she to her inmost bower, and there
Unclaspï¿½d the wedded eagles of her belt,
The grim Earlï¿½s gift; but ever at a breath
She lingerï¿½d, looking like a summer moon
Half-dipt in cloud: anon she shook her head,
And showerï¿½d the rippled ringlets to her knee;
Unclad herself in haste; adown the stair
Stole on; and, like a creeping sunbeam, slid
From pillar unto pillar, until she reachï¿½d
The gateway; there she found her palfrey trapt
In purple blazonï¿½d with armorial gold.
Then she rode forth, clothed on with chastity:
The deep air listenï¿½d round her as she rode,
And all the low wind hardly breathed for fear.
The little wide-mouthï¿½d heads upon the spout
Had cunning eyes to see: the barking cur
Made her cheek flame: her palfreyï¿½s footfall shot
Light horrors throï¿½ her pulses: the blind walls
Were full of chinks and holes; and overhead
Fantastic gables, crowding, stared: but she
Not less throï¿½ all bore up, till, last, she saw
The white-flowerï¿½d elder-thicket from the field
Gleam throï¿½ the Gothic archways in the wall.
Then she rode back clothï¿½d on with chastity:
And one low churl, compact of thankless earth,
The fatal byword of all years to come,
Boring a little auger-hole in fear,
Peepï¿½dï¿½but his eyes, before they had their will,
Were shrivellï¿½d into darkness in his head,
And dropt before him. So the Powers, who wait
On noble deeds, cancellï¿½d a sense misused;
And she, that knew not, passï¿½d: and all at once,
With twelve great shocks of sound, the shameless noon
Was clashï¿½d and hammerï¿½d from a hundred towers,
One after one: but even then she gainï¿½d
Her bower; whence reissuing, robed and crownï¿½d,
To meet her lord, she took the tax away,
And built herself an everlasting name.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Alfred Lord Tennyson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Alfred Lord Tennyson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
